nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to Lake Towuti in the Malili lake system of central Sulawesi; lives on hard rocky substrates along submerged shorelines and rubble fields. Adults are most commonly observed on rocks at depths of approximately 3-5 m where the water is warm, alkaline, and exceptionally stable year-round. The species shelters in crevices between basalt and limestone rocks and forages on biofilm-coated surfaces.

decorPreferences: inert rocky hardscape (basalt, lava rock, limestone, ceramic rubble) providing crevices and biofilm-grazing surfaces
inert substrate such as crushed coral or aragonite sand for pH/kH buffering
minimal or no live planting — most aquarium plants struggle in the warm alkaline parameters; tolerant species such as Anubias, Bucephalandra, Java fern, or Sulawesi-origin macrophytes may be used
gentle, well-oxygenated flow via sponge filter or low-output internal filter
tight-fitting lid (Sulawesi shrimp can crawl across damp surfaces in distress)
low to moderate lighting to encourage biofilm growth on rockwork

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on biofilm, aufwuchs (microbial film and microalgae) growing on rock surfaces in its native lake. In captivity it accepts powdered shrimp foods, biofilm boosters (Bacter AE and similar), high-quality micro-pellets, and occasional blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini, mulberry leaf) in very small portions. Indian almond leaf, mulberry leaf, and other botanical leaf litter help generate the microbial film that constitutes the bulk of the natural diet. Feed sparingly — these shrimp are slow grazers and easily polluted by overfeeding. A mature, well-aged tank with established biofilm is more important than feeding frequency.

goodWith: conspecifics (groups of 10+ strongly recommended; 15-25+ ideal for stable colony behaviour)
other Sulawesi Caridina species sharing the same parameters (C. dennerli, C. woltereckae, C. striata, C. profundicola)
small peaceful Sulawesi-origin fish kept at the same parameters (with caution and in larger tanks)
avoidWith: all fish in small tanks — even peaceful species will outcompete shrimp for food and stress them
any species requiring soft, acidic water (incompatible parameters)
Neocaridina (e.g. cherry shrimp) and lowland Caridina (e.g. crystal/bee shrimp) — different parameter requirements; do not crossbreed
fin-nippers, cichlids, loaches, and any predatory fish
copper-based medications and copper-containing fertilisers (acutely toxic)

summary: Tiny, jewel-like freshwater shrimp endemic to Lake Towuti in central Sulawesi. Typically marketed as 'Harlequin' or 'Yellow Nose' Sulawesi shrimp — the trade name overlaps with several closely related Lake Towuti Caridina. Requires warm, hard, alkaline water with rock-stable parameters and a mature tank; not a beginner species.
careNotes:
Caridina spinata is a small (commonly cited at 1.2-1.8 cm body length, with some sources reporting females up to 1.8 cm and males around 1.5 cm) freshwater shrimp endemic to Lake Towuti in the Malili lake system of central Sulawesi, Indonesia. It is classified Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List, with the most cited threats being the spread of the invasive flowerhorn cichlid (predicted >90% population decline within a decade), eutrophication, nickel-mining pollution, and over-collection for the aquarium trade. Most shrimp in the trade are wild-caught.

Common-name disambiguation: In the freshwater hobby, 'Harlequin Sulawesi Shrimp' is more often applied to Caridina woltereckae (a sympatric Lake Towuti species with stronger purple-red and white banding). Caridina spinata is more accurately the 'Yellow Nose' or 'Yellow Goldflake' shrimp, characterised by yellow markings on the rostrum and cheeks plus light-coloured bands across a dark body. The two species are sometimes sold interchangeably under the 'Harlequin' name. There is ALSO a completely unrelated saltwater Harlequin Shrimp (Hymenocera picta) found under the same English common name in marine retail — verify water type and scientific name before purchase.

Water chemistry is the central husbandry challenge. The Malili lakes are warm (around 27-29 degC year-round), alkaline (pH around 8.0-8.5), moderately hard (gH 6-8, kH 4-6), and exceptionally stable. Reproducing these conditions almost always requires building tank water from RO/DI with a Sulawesi-specific remineralisation salt (e.g. Salty Shrimp Sulawesi Mineral 8.5). Inert substrates only (crushed coral or aragonite sand to maintain buffering) — do not use buffering ('active') aquasoils intended for Caridina bee shrimp, which drive pH down and will kill Sulawesi shrimp. Tanks should be matured for 4-6 months before introducing stock so a robust biofilm establishes.

Keep in groups of at least 10, with 15-25+ strongly recommended. Sulawesi shrimp are noticeably shyer than Neocaridina; in small numbers or in mixed-species tanks with fish, they will hide constantly. A species-only tank greatly improves visibility and reduces mortality. Stability is more important than absolute parameter values — abrupt temperature, pH, or kH swings cause mass mortality. Drip-acclimate over several hours when introducing new stock. Care is widely parallel to Caridina dennerli (Cardinal Shrimp) from Lake Matano.

Copper sensitivity: as with all dwarf shrimp, copper-based medications, copper-bearing plant fertilisers, and copper plumbing residues are acutely toxic. Many planted-tank trace-mix fertilisers contain copper and should be avoided. Source sensitivity note: cross-source spread on temperature was wide (25-30 degC across sources; aquariumbreeder cites 28.7-29.2 degC as natural lake conditions, while shrimpkeepers and OC Shrimps quote a broader hobbyist range of 25-30 degC), pH (7.8-8.5), and group-size minimum (10 vs 15-25); averaged conservative middles were chosen.
breedingNotes: Difficult in captivity. Like other Lake Towuti Caridina, C. spinata produces small clutches of relatively large eggs (sources cite low fecundity, on the order of 15-30 eggs per clutch with extended gestation typical of large-egg Sulawesi Caridina) that hatch directly into miniature shrimp (no planktonic larval stage). Successful breeding requires a long-established species-only tank with rock-stable parameters, abundant biofilm, and no competing tankmates. Juveniles graze immediately on biofilm; supplemental feeding of powdered shrimp food can be helpful. Growth is slow. Hobbyist-scale captive reproduction is achievable but population growth is sluggish compared with Neocaridina or lowland Caridina, and the species remains predominantly wild-collected in the trade.

primary.name: Aquarium Breeder — Sulawesi Harlequin Shrimp Detailed Guide
primary.notes: Used as functional primary because the supplied Flip Aquatics collection URL returned 404 and The Shrimp Farm root returned 403. Provided adult size (~1.5-1.8 cm), natural temperature (~28.7-29.2 degC) and broader tolerance, pH 8.4-8.5, gH 6-7, kH 4-5, tank-size floor (~20-40 L), lifespan (1-2 years), native range (Lake Towuti, Malili lake system), copper sensitivity, peaceful temperament, and breeding-difficulty category. Article hedges between C. spinata and the sympatric C. woltereckae under the 'Harlequin' trade name.

additional.name: ShrimpKeepers — Sulawesi Shrimp Care Guide (Yellow Nose / Caridina spinata)
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (target 28-29 degC, broader Sulawesi range 25-30 degC), pH (8.0-8.5), gH (6-8), kH (4-6), tank size (10-20 gal / ~38-76 L), group size (15-25+ for colony stability), diet (aufwuchs, biofilm, Bacter AE), breeding difficulty (Hard, low fecundity 10-20 eggs/clutch), and the substrate-and-remineraliser protocol (inert crushed-coral substrate + Salty Shrimp Sulawesi Mineral 8.5, 4-6 month tank maturation). Explicitly identifies the species as Caridina spinata sold as 'Yellow Nose'.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Caridina spinata
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Atyidae, Decapoda), authorship (Woltereck, 1937), Lake Towuti endemism, rocky substrate habitat, adult depth preference 3-5 m, alternative common name 'yellow goldflake shrimp', and IUCN Critically Endangered classification. Documented threats: invasive flowerhorn cichlids, eutrophication, nickel-mining pollution, and aquarium-trade collection.

additional.name: Aqualog Lexikon — Caridina sp. Malili Yellow Nose (= C. cf. spinata)
additional.notes: European trade-source cross-check confirming Sulawesi origin, temperature 27-30 degC, pH around 8.0, body size 1.0-1.5 cm, omnivorous diet, group-keeping recommendation, and minimum tank length 40-80 cm. Lists the species under the 'Malili Yellow Nose' trade label, which is commonly applied to C. spinata.

additional.name: Atyidae.wordpress.com — Caridina spongicola and Caridina woltereckae
additional.notes: Taxonomic disambiguation reference. Confirms that C. woltereckae is the species more accurately matching the 'Harlequin' name (stronger purple-red and white banding, widely distributed in Lake Towuti) and that hobby naming between C. spinata and C. woltereckae is frequently muddled. Informs the alsoKnownAs list and common-name collision warning in careNotes.

additional.name: OC Shrimp Aquatic — Sulawesi Shrimp product page
additional.notes: US trade source confirming temperature 25-30 degC, pH 7.8-8.4, gH 6-8 dGH, kH 4-6 dKH, minimum tank 10 gal (~38 L), and copper sensitivity. The page captions the listing 'Harlequin Shrimp' but notes the supplied species varies; used for parameter cross-check rather than identification.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molts periodically as it grows; published interval estimates are not species-specific. Stable kH (4-6) and gH (6-8) are critical — kH swings cause molting failures (the 'white ring of death' / incomplete exoskeleton split) that are usually fatal. Leave shed exoskeletons in the tank; the shrimp will consume them to recover calcium.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Direct-developing (no planktonic larvae); produces small clutches of large eggs typical of Lake Towuti Caridina. Requires a long-established species-only tank with rock-stable parameters and abundant biofilm. Reproduction is slow.
